Glossary About
South Asian debates on mind · c. 700 BCE – 12th c. CE

South Asian Traditions

South Asian philosophy develops some of the richest premodern arguments anywhere about consciousness, selfhood, perception, desire, error, liberation, and the relation between mind and world.

The materials gathered here do not form a single school. They include Upaniṣadic speculation on ātman and brahman; Nyāya realism about self and valid cognition; Sāṅkhya and Yoga analyses of witness-consciousness and mental discipline; Buddhist critiques of self and intrinsic nature; and Jain accounts of conscious soul, karmic obstruction, and many-sided truth.

What makes the cluster so important for a history of philosophy of mind is the density of the shared problem-space. These thinkers ask whether consciousness is reducible to mental process, whether the self is substantial or constructed, whether perception is direct or concept-laden, how memory and habit shape agency, and whether liberation requires the transformation, purification, or dissolution of ordinary subjectivity.

The page below is organized as a navigational threshold into those debates. Some entries are individuals, some are canonical corpora, and some are schools whose arguments unfold across centuries. Together they extend the site beyond its earlier Mediterranean and modern arc and make the comparative history of mind considerably fuller.

c. 700 – 200 BCE

Upaniṣads

Ordinary mind as instrument, consciousness as the deeper condition of experience.

c. 2nd c. BCE – 2nd c. CE

Gautama

Nyāya’s canonical author on self, perception, doubt, and the causal structure of cognition.

2nd c. CE onward

Nyāya

From Gautama to Gaṅgeśa: realism, inference, universals, and the enduring self.

c. 4th c. CE

Īśvarakṛṣṇa

Sāṅkhya’s witness-consciousness dualism and its layered account of mind as subtle nature.

c. 2nd c. BCE – 4th c. CE

Patañjali

A therapeutic psychology of attention, habit, affliction, and meditative absorption.

c. 150 – 250 CE

Nāgārjuna

Emptiness, dependent arising, and the refusal of fixed mental or metaphysical substances.

4th – 5th c. CE

Vasubandhu

One of the great Buddhist theorists of representation, self-modeling, and conditioned consciousness.

c. 480 – 540 CE

Dignāga

Nonconceptual perception, exclusion-theory semantics, and reflexive awareness.

c. 600 – 660 CE

Dharmakīrti

Momentary cognition, self-awareness, conceptual construction, and the problem of other minds.

8th c. CE

Śaṅkara

Mind as instrument, consciousness as self-luminous, and ignorance as misidentification.

725 – 788 CE

Śāntarakṣita

Conventionally mind-only, ultimately empty: cognition without intrinsic nature.

c. 2nd c. CE

Kundakunda

The soul as conscious substance, seen from ultimate and conventional standpoints.

c. 2nd – 4th c. CE

Umāsvāti

The soul’s cognitive activity, obstructed by karmic matter and clarified through discipline.

8th c. CE

Haribhadra

Comparative philosophy and a soteriological psychology of delusion, discipline, and insight.

1088 – 1172

Hemacandra

Jain epistemology and moral psychology in a high scholastic register.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
